text.title: Heuristic Evaluation Process and Nielsen's 10 Heuristics
text.summary: Six-step heuristic evaluation process, the full heuristic set with core questions, evaluator requirements, and the 0-4 severity scale.

list.item: #1 Visibility of system status — does the user always know what is happening?
#2 Match between system and real world — does it use user language?
#3 User control and freedom — can the user undo and exit?
#4 Consistency and standards — is behavior predictable?
#5 Error prevention — does it prevent mistakes before they happen?
#6 Recognition over recall — are options visible rather than remembered?
#7 Flexibility and efficiency of use — does it support expert shortcuts?
#8 Aesthetic and minimalist design — is the interface clutter-free?
#9 Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors — are errors helpful?
#10 Help and documentation — is help available when needed?

rule.statement: Use 3-5 evaluators. Each evaluator works independently before any group discussion. Combining findings happens only after all individual evaluations are complete.
rule.rationale: 3-5 evaluators find ~75% of usability issues; a single evaluator finds ~35%. Independent evaluation prevents anchoring bias — if evaluators discuss first, later evaluators' findings are biased toward earlier findings. Independence maximizes coverage.

rule.statement: Run one heuristic per evaluation pass. Do not attempt to evaluate all 10 heuristics simultaneously in one walkthrough.
rule.rationale: Single-heuristic focus produces 3x more findings per heuristic than omnibus evaluation. Evaluating all 10 at once produces shallow coverage of each. Dedicated passes allow deeper attention and catch issues missed by divided attention.

rule.statement: Rate every finding on a 0-4 severity scale. Use this scale consistently for prioritization: severity 4 blocks release, severity 3 is a sprint-level fix, severity 1-2 goes to the polish backlog.
rule.rationale: Without consistent severity ratings, all issues appear equally urgent and teams cannot prioritize. The 0-4 scale (from Nielsen 1994) provides a shared vocabulary for comparing findings across evaluators and cycles.

list.item: 1: Cosmetic — fix only if extra time available
3: Major — important to fix, high priority
4: Catastrophic — imperative to fix before release

rule.statement: Step 1: Define scope — which screens/flows to evaluate, which heuristics to focus on, which task scenarios to walk through.
rule.rationale: Undefined scope leads to surface-level review. Task scenarios anchor evaluation to realistic usage rather than abstract interface inspection.

rule.statement: Step 2: Gather materials — interface or prototype access, task scenarios, evaluation template, screen recording if possible.
rule.rationale: Consistent materials across evaluators ensure comparable findings. Screen recordings allow revisiting observations during compilation.

rule.statement: Steps 3-4: Each evaluator independently walks through the interface against each heuristic, documents findings with location, heuristic, problem description, severity, and recommendation. Then compile: remove duplicates, aggregate severity ratings (average where they differ, note range).
rule.rationale: Compilation after independent evaluation preserves diversity of findings while removing noise. Averaging severity ratings across evaluators reduces individual bias.

rule.statement: Step 6: Report findings with concrete location references (screen name, component, flow step) and specific recommendations. "UI is confusing" is not an actionable finding.
rule.rationale: Vague findings cannot be acted on. A finding without a location cannot be reproduced. A finding without a recommendation leaves remediation to chance. All three elements are required for each finding to be actionable.
